Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd maybe sit down with my laptop when I felt like it, but it wasn't consistent at all. Sometimes I'd watch a bunch of videos in one sitting, other times I'd go days without touching anything course-related. I learn better by doing things with my hands anyway, so sitting and reading or watching lectures felt tedious. I'd often end up scrolling through my phone or doing something else while the videos played in the background.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. I'd cram before deadlines and then basically disappear from the course for a while. Regular study times? That sounds nice in theory, but between my extracurriculars and just not feeling that motivated, it never happened. I'd tell myself I'd study on Tuesdays and Thursdays, but that plan lasted maybe one week.
Q3: Usually it was because I remembered an assignment was due soon, or I'd get a notification that something was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I was missing anything important, scroll around for a bit, then leave. It wasn't like I was excited to be there or anything. Boredom sometimes drove me there too, ironically—like I had nothing better to do so I might as well see what's happening in the course.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. It never became a habit for me. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I was logging in more regularly because assignments were piling up, but once that pressure passed, I'd drift away again. The LMS just felt like another thing I had to deal with rather than something I wanted to engage with.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful because I could watch them at my own pace and rewind if I zoned out. I also appreciated when there were interactive elements or anything that let me actually do something rather than just passively absorb information. The discussion boards were okay when people actually had interesting things to say, but that was rare.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the readings and a lot of the supplementary materials. They felt dry and I just couldn't bring myself to sit through pages of text. I'd skim them at best or just skip them entirely if I thought I could get by without them. The optional resources? Forget it—if it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it.
Q7: It had to feel relevant or at least somewhat interesting. If I could see how it connected to something practical or if it was presented in an engaging way, I'd pay attention. But if it felt like busywork or something I'd never use again, I couldn't be bothered. I need to see the point of what I'm doing or my brain just checks out.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore. Required ones at least had some weight to them because they affected my grade. But even with required stuff, if it seemed low-stakes, I'd put in minimal effort. Optional things? I'd glance at them and think 'that's nice' and move on with my life.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides or PDFs and study from those on my tablet when I was out. Sometimes I'd look things up on YouTube if the course explanations weren't clicking for me. I also had a study group through one of my extracurriculars where we'd go over material together, and none of that showed up in the LMS obviously.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was reviewing downloaded notes or working through practice problems on paper. I'm kinesthetic, so I learn better when I'm writing things out or physically engaging with material. I'd also discuss concepts with friends over coffee, which helped me understand things way better than staring at a screen.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once, maybe download the slides, and then never go back to the LMS for that topic. I'd study from my notes or the downloaded files instead. It just felt easier than logging back in and navigating through everything again.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where I just couldn't bring myself to care. Usually when the material got really dry or when I had more exciting things going on with my clubs and social life. The boredom would hit hard and I'd just mentally check out. Sometimes I'd go through the motions but not actually absorb anything.
Q13: Oh for sure. When I had events or commitments for my extracurriculars, the course took a backseat. I'd also get fatigued from staring at screens all day since I was taking so many online courses. By the time I got to this one, I was often just too tired to engage properly. Competing responsibilities definitely won out most of the time.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times. I'd have a video playing but I'd be thinking about something else entirely. Or I'd scroll through the discussion posts without really reading them. My body was present but my mind was somewhere else. It felt like going through the motions just to say I did something.
Q15: Constantly. I'd put off the readings because they bored me to tears. Even assignments I knew were important, I'd wait until the last possible moment. I think part of it was that I just didn't feel that invested in the course, so everything felt like a chore. Procrastination was my default mode.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd suddenly become very active in the LMS right before something was due. It's not a great system, I know, but the pressure of a deadline was the only thing that could cut through my boredom and make me actually do the work.
Q17: Not really, to be honest. I'd read feedback and think 'okay, noted' but it didn't change my behavior much. If I got a decent grade, I'd feel validated in my minimal effort approach. If I got critical feedback, I'd feel a bit annoyed but not motivated enough to change anything.
Q18: The structure was pretty standard, which meant it was easy to figure out what I could skip and still pass. Having flexibility was nice in theory but it also meant I could drift away without immediate consequences. A more rigid structure might have kept me more on track, though I probably would have resented it.
Q19: The lack of interactive or hands-on elements made it harder for me to stay engaged. I'm not a sit-and-watch kind of learner. When there were more dynamic activities or things I could actually participate in, I was more likely to pay attention. The heavy reliance on videos and readings didn't suit my learning style at all.
Q20: I think some students might be exploring the platform, watching videos, checking things out, but not actually committing to the work. Maybe they're anxious about submitting something imperfect, or they're trying to learn but struggling to produce output. Or they're like me—present but not really engaged, just going through the motions.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. I didn't log in that much or participate heavily in discussions, but I managed to pass with decent grades. I studied offline a lot and crammed effectively before exams. The LMS activity doesn't capture what happens outside the platform, and some of us just work differently.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or learning anything. They miss offline studying, conversations with peers, and whether the material actually stuck. They're just tracking behavior, not understanding or engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and being mentally present. It's not just showing up or clicking through things—it's when you're curious and invested. For me, that's rare in academic settings. I feel engaged when something captures my interest, but that doesn't happen often with coursework.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how bored I was most of the time, or how much I relied on last-minute cramming and offline studying. It wouldn't capture my conversations with friends about the material or the fact that I was juggling a lot of other commitments. My engagement looked higher on paper than it felt in reality.
